coredata.dc:title: How do carbon dots with amine terminals help perovskite solar cells perform better?
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Zhao
Zhang, Xiaoyu
Wang, Chen
Zhang, Wei
Zheng, Weitao
coredata.dc:description: Lead halide perovskite solar cells can be significantly improved with multifunctional additives such as carbon dots (CDs) with amine terminals via defect passivation and band alignment. Here, we determine rational guidelines for the usage of such quantum dots in perovskite solar cells by systematically studying their effects on crystal quality and carrier transport. The amine-terminated CDs homogeneously dispersed in precursors improve the crystal quality of perovskite films, but they reduce the hole mobility hampering hole extraction. In contrast, CDs deposited between the electron transport layer and active layer could promote spin-coated film quality and electron’s extraction efficiently. So, the approaches of the carbon dots in device alters semiconductor properties and energy level structure. Interface engineering achieve better crystal quality and energy level alignment, which in turn affects charge collection, thereby increasing current density and power conversion efficiency. According to the findings of this study, when considering defect passivation by additives, the energy band alignment of the device should also be considered, since this could change carrier dynamics and device performance differently.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Perovskite
Defect passivation
Energy band alignment
Solar cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis and characterization of CDs
The effects of PE and IE on device performance
The effects of CDs on perovskite carrier dynamics
Device physics of IE-derived solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51972136 and 52072141 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Device structure diagram. (b) Current density-voltage curves of the PE device with 0, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 mg/mL CDs. (c) Current density-voltage curves of the IE device with 0, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 mg/mL CDs.
(a)-(d) Top view SEM images of the PE films with 0, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04 mg/mL CDs. (e)-(g) SEM images of the IE films with 0.02, 0.03, 0.04 mg/mL CDs.

(a) X-ray diffraction pattern and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of PE, IE, and control perovskite films. (c) FTIR spectra of CDs, and PbI2 with and without CDs. (d) XPS spectra of PE, IE, and control perovskite films. (e) Steady-state PL spectra and (f) TRPL curves of PE, IE, and control perovskite films.
(a) UPS of IE, PE, and perovskite films, untreated. Diagram of the energy level alignment of (b) IE, (c) PE, and (d) control perovskite films.

(a) Steady-state current output and power output at the maximum power point of IE and control devices. (b) EQE curves, (c) J
SC statistical distribution of IE and control devices. (d) TRPL curves of the IE and control perovskite films on ETL. (e) EIS and (f) SCLC measurements of IE and control devices.
Long-term shelf stability of the unencapsulated PE, IE and control devices. The PCE value were normalized in the spectrum.
Photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs under 1 sun (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2).

head.ce:title: How do carbon dots with amine terminals help perovskite solar cells perform better?

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: KeyLaboratory of Mobile Materials MOE State Key Laboratory of Automotive Simulation and Control Jilin University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials MOE, School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials MOE, School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Electron Microscopy Center and School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electron Microscopy Center and School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead halide perovskite solar cells can be significantly improved with multifunctional additives such as carbon dots (CDs) with amine terminals via defect passivation and band alignment. Here, we determine rational guidelines for the usage of such quantum dots in perovskite solar cells by systematically studying their effects on crystal quality and carrier transport. The amine-terminated CDs homogeneously dispersed in precursors improve the crystal quality of perovskite films, but they reduce the hole mobility hampering hole extraction. In contrast, CDs deposited between the electron transport layer and active layer could promote spin-coated film quality and electron’s extraction efficiently. So, the approaches of the carbon dots in device alters semiconductor properties and energy level structure. Interface engineering achieve better crystal quality and energy level alignment, which in turn affects charge collection, thereby increasing current density and power conversion efficiency. According to the findings of this study, when considering defect passivation by additives, the energy band alignment of the device should also be considered, since this could change carrier dynamics and device performance differently.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) and perovskite light-emitting diodes (Pe-LED) have seen significant advances over the last decade, including a certified record power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 25.8 % for normal n-i-p structured PSCs and a record external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 28.1 % for green Pe-LEDs [1–3]. The crucial factors of these remarkable progresses were the high-quality perovskite film and optimized energy level structure, which could be both accomplished with additives engineering. Additives engineering could passivate defects, promote crystallization, orienting crystal growth, enlarging crystal sizes. Besides chemical interaction, additives engineering could also enhance carrier collection and block reverse carrier transport. Various additives have been employed, such as inorganic salts, small molecules, polymers, and zero-dimensional (0D) nanoparticles [4,5]. Among these above additives, 0D nanoparticles have abundant designable surface chemistry active sites, which can "kill two birds or even more birds with one stone" to achieve multiple optimizations of perovskite devices. 0D nanoparticles could affect the crystallization by providing heterogeneous nucleation sites and specifically distributed on crystal surfaces or grain boundary. In addition, 0D nanoparticles suggest good thermal stability compared with the small molecules. Therefore, 0D nanoparticles have been extensively studied as additives in perovskite optoelectronic devices.

A unique 0D nanoparticle called carbon dots (CDs) has been considered a potential candidate for improving the performance of perovskites due to its low cost, facile synthesis, and easily tunable physical properties [6,7]. Various functional groups of CDs from multiple chemical synthesis routes could adjust perovskite crystallization and passivate crystal defects. CDs can enhance the performance of perovskite solar cells as either a precursor additive or an interface modifier [8–12]. The CDs dispersed into the perovskite precursor could not only achieve morphology tuning, but also achieve grain boundary filling after crystallization. Guo et al. introduced carbonized bamboo-derived carbon dots and urea into the precursor to modulate crystal growth and achieve stable grain boundaries through passivation [13]. Chen and co-workers modified perovskite grains with CDs to enhance carrier transport, which improved device efficiency [11]. Meanwhile CDs optimized energy level alignment as interfacial modifier and promoted carrier transfer at the interface. And the function groups on the CDs could also passivate defects suppressing non-radiative recombination at interfaces [14]. Zhu et al. introduced an ultrathin CDs layer between the perovskite and electron transport layer to achieve superfast electron extraction [7]. Huang’s and Yang’s group combined CDs with ETL to enable energy level matching and reduce trap states at the interface, respectively [6,15]. We also previously investigated CDs for manipulating perovskite crystal growth and promoting carrier transport in perovskite solar cells and LEDs [16,17].

Even though it has been proven that CDs can improve the performance of perovskite devices, a rational experimental design and systematic experimental studies are still needed to figure out how to balance carrier transport and film quality improvement to maximize the utility of CDs. Here, amine-terminated carbon dots (CDs in short) were selected as study objects to systematically investigate the effect of CDs on the passivation and carrier transport characteristics of perovskite films via precursor engineering (PE) and interface engineering (IE). Amines with electron donating ability are expected to manipulate the perovskite film morphology and carrier dynamics. Results show that IE devices perform better, but PE devices suggest lower power conversion efficiency (PCE). We compared the properties of PE, IE, and control perovskite films to investigate the cause. The CDs in PE and IE both achieve crystallization manipulating and defect passivation by interacting with uncoordinated Pb atom. PE raises the perovskite energy level and affects the mobility of hole leading to more carrier recombination, whereas IE could coordinate with defect and achieve energy-level alignment without introducing carrier recombination sites. Consequently, PCE of CH3NH3PbI3 devices with IE increased from 18.1 % to 19.6 % after defect passivation and energy level alignment. In spite of both IE and PE suggesting positive effects on the morphology and defects passivation, the carrier dynamics should be carefully designed otherwise to take full advantage of the excellent semiconducting property.

Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.0 %), were purchased from Aladdin. The PbI2, PbBr2, methylamine iodide (MAI), formamidinium iodide (FAI), methylamium bromide (MABr), 4-tert-Butylpyridine (TBP, 98 %) and (2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis-(N, N-di-p-methoxyphenylamine)–9,9′-spirobifluorene, 99.8 %) Spiro-OMeTAD were bought from Xi’an Polymer Corp. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and bis (trifluoromethyl sulfonyl) amine lithium salt (Li-TFSI, 99.95 %) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Corp. Chlorobenzene and acetonitrile were purchased from J&K Scientific Corp. Indium tin oxide (ITO) glasses were bought from Shenzhen South China Xiangcheng Technology Corp. LTD.

The amine-terminated carbon dots (CDs) was prepared according to the previous report [17]. Briefly, the CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and ethylenediamine. After purification and frozen-dry treatment, the CDs powder was dissolved and diluted with DMF/DMSO to different concentrations.

The synthesis process is according to the earlier report [18]. Briefly, SnCl2·2 H2O and thiourea with the mass ratio of 3:1 were dissolved in the deionized water in the conical flask, under the vigorous magnetic stirring at room temperature for 24 h.

The CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite precursor was prepared by dissolving 149 mg MAI and 461 mg PbI2 in 0.83 mL DMF/DMSO (4/1, v/v). The CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite precursor with CDs was prepared by mixing the perovskite precursor and different concentration CDs solution (20/1, v/v). The Cs0.05(MA0.17FA0.83)0.95Pb(Br0.17I0.83)3 mixed-cation perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 507.1 mg PbI2, 73.4 mg PbBr2, 172 mg FAI, and 21.2 mg MABr in 1 mL mixture of DMF and DMSO (4:1, v/v). After the solid was totally dissolved, 40 μL of CsI solution (1.5 M in DMSO) was added to the mixture. After stirring at 70 °C for 30 min, the precursor solution was ready to use.

ITO glass substrates were successively cleaned by ultrasonic washing with surfactant, acetone and isopropanol then blow-dried with dry nitrogen. Then, the substrates were treated with UV plasma for 15 min before next steps. The SnO2 layers were deposited on the substrates by spin-coating at 3000 rpm and annealing at 180 ℃ in muffle furnace for 1 h. The perovskite precursor was spin-coated at 1000 rpm for 10 s and 5000 rpm for 25 s with a drop of anti-solution (chlorobenzene and 6 % acetonitrile) at 16 s prior to the end of spinning program. The spin-coated films were immediately transferred to hotplates and annealed at 70 ℃ for 2 mins and 110 ℃ for 10 mins to completely evaporate the solvent. The interface engineering devices were fabricated by spin-coating CDs solution at different concentrations at 2000 rpm and then heated at 110 ℃ for 10 min before perovskite deposition.

Mixed-cation perovskite devices were fabricated by a two-step spin-coating program, 1000 rpm for 10 s and 4000 rpm for 40 s. During the second spin-coating step 300 μL chlorobenzene was quickly dropped on the spinning substrate. After spin-coating, the films were annealed at 60 °C for 5 min and 140 °C for 10 min. The hole transport layer (HTL) was prepared by the spin casting of the solution on top of the perovskite layer at 4000 rpm for 30 s. The Spiro-OMeTAD solution was prepared by dissolving 72.6 mg Spiro-OMeTAD in 1 mL chlorobenzene and mixing with 17.6 μL of a 520 mg/mL solution of lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-imide (LiTFSI) in acetonitrile and 28.8 μL of 4-tert-butylpyridine (tBP). Perovskite and Spiro-OMeTAD deposition processes should all be carried out in a N2-filled glovebox. The glovebox temperature needs to stay at ∼ 20 °C. Finally, 90 nm Ag electrodes were deposited by thermal evaporation at 1 Å/s sequentially.

The current density–voltage measurements (J-V curves) were obtained with a Keithley 2400 source meter under simulated sunlight irradiation at AM 1.5 100 mW/cm2. The scan delay time was 30 ms, and the scan speed was 0.1 V/s. The EQE curves were characterized by Crowntech QTest Station 1000 AD. As for CDs TEM measurement A double Cs-corrected TEM (JEM ARM300F GRANDARM) operated at a voltage of 300 kV was employed, equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on IS50 FT-IR. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured with D8 Advance-Bruker AXS X system. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with field emission scanning electron microscopy (SU8010, Hitachi) at an acceleration voltage of 4 kV. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) were measured by PREVAC system. Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) images were taken from AFM500II (Hitachi). The UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out by using UV–vis spectrophotometer-1700 (Shimadzu). Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) of perovskite film was excited by a laser at 405 nm and measured by Ocean Optics fiber optic spectrometer USB4000. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) curves were measured by FLS920P Edinburgh spectrometer. The buried interface fabrications were referred to the early report [19]. First, the perovskite films were spin-coated on the PTAA substrates, then 200 nm Cu were deposited by thermal evaporation. When the PTAA films were dissolved by chlorobenzene, the buried interfaces were exposed and the floating perovskite films could be collected. After multiple flushes and drying, the perovskite films with buried interface could be used for measurement.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted by using the 1J6520B precision impedance spectrum analyzer (Admiral) with the frequency range from 10 Hz to 1 MHz in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots (CDs) for interface engineering (IE) and precursor engineering (PE) were prepared from a hydrothermal and carbonization reaction between citric acid and ethane diamine. The as-prepared CDs were dissolved in 2-methoxyethanol and DMF for IE and PE, respectively. According to transmission electron microscope (TEM) images in Fig. S1a, the CDs are monodispersed with an average size of 3 nm. Using atomic force microscopy (AFM), the thickness of CD was measured approximately as 3 nm (Figs. S1b and 1c), indicating that these particles are quasi-spherical with diameters of approximately 3 nm. Fig. S1d shows the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the CDs. In accordance with previous CD spectra reports, two absorption peaks are observed at 260 nm and 360 nm, while the PL peak is detected at 445 nm. The elemental composition of CDs is confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S1e, the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum for C 1 s of CDs suggests three components corresponding to C–C (∼283.6 eV), CO (∼286.9 eV), C–N (∼285.1 eV). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to analyze the surface functional group on CDs. Fig. S1f shows peaks at 1650, 1600, and 1340 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and C–N, respectively. Meanwhile, a broad peak at 3400 cm−1 and a relatively small peak at 2950 cm−1 proved N–H presence on the CD surface [20]. Based on the above analysis, it could be concluded that CD surfaces are mainly terminated by –NH2 functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of PE and IE on device performance
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CDs were evaluated using CH3NH3PbI3 perovskites by precursor and interface engineering, where the CDs were added to the precursor solution or precoated on the substrate. To investigate the effect of CDs on different application, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with a structure of ITO/SnO2/CH3NH3PbI3/Spiro-MeOTAD/Au as shown in Fig. 1a have been manufactured.

The control device without any treatment showed a champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 17.81 %, which can be derived from a short-circuit current density (J
sc ) of 21.62 mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (V
oc ) of 1.07 V, and a fill factor (FF) of 76 %. Fig. 1b shows the current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the PE devices with different CDs concentrations and Table 1 summarizes the corresponding photovoltaic parameters. The trend in performance implies that CDs precursor engineering leads to a significant degradation in performance. With the 0.02 mg/mL CDs, the best PE device had a PCE of 15.85 %. As a contrast, the performances of the PSCs with CDs interface engineering are shown in Fig. 1c. The optimized IE PSCs with 0.03 mg/mL CDs showed the best performance, with significantly higher current density than the control device. The champion device suggests a J
sc of 22.81 mA/cm2, a V
oc of 1.09 V, an FF of 79 % and an efficiency of 19.60 %. The highest efficiency of PE devices suggests 23.7 % enhancement compared to the control device. The optimized IE devices also displayed the highest J
sc , V
oc , and FF among the three kinds of devices.

The top view scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to investigate the effects of PE and IE on perovskite morphologies. Fig. 2a–d show uniform and dense polycrystalline perovskite grains. Comparing to control films, the average grain size of 0.02 and 0.03 mg/mL PE films significantly increased from 340 nm to 360 nm and 460 nm, respectively. However, excessive CDs resulted in smaller grain sizes (265 nm). IE films also exhibited larger grain sizes than control films, as shown in Fig. 2e–g.

As shown in Fig. 3a, the XRD patterns suggest typical patterns of perovskites with characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to perovskite (110) (2θ ≈ 14.15°) and (220) (2θ ≈ 28.5°), which are in accordance with previous results [21,22]. There was no change in the perovskite phase or crystallization caused by the introduction of CDs. As a result of the increased intensity of diffraction peaks, both PE and IE improve perovskite crystallinity. In the meantime, the decreased full-width at half maximum is correlated with enlarged grain size. The UV–vis absorption spectra of PE, IE, and control films are shown in Fig. 3b. In accordance with higher crystallinity and larger grain sizes, IE perovskite films show higher absorption intensities than control films. It seems that the increase in absorption intensity in PE films is less obvious than in IE. The higher absorption intensities could be both explained by better crystal quality.

The chemical effects of PE and IE are also considered along with perovskite morphology and crystallinity. To investigate the interaction between CDs and perovskite, Fig. 3c compares the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of CDs and CDs mixed with PbI2. The N–H bond stretching vibration peak occurs at 3250 cm−1 in CDs and shifts to 3350 cm−1 in CDs mixed with PbI2. The peak shift to a higher wavenumber suggests a more evenly distributed cloud density indicating that –NH2 as a Lewis base provides a lonely electron pair to Pb2+ [23]. It is possible for the amine groups in the CDs to form coordination bonds with non-stoichiometric PbI2 or Pb2+. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has proved the interaction between CDs and perovskites. As XPS measurements only have a shallow detection depth, we compared the XPS signals of the buried interface with IE and PE. Fig. 3d illustrates that the Pb characteristic peaks in PE and IE films are both shifting towards lower binding energies. The shift in peak position indicates that –NH2 on CDs donated electron pairs to the uncoordinated lead atoms, which is consistent with the FTIR results [24,25]. These results confirm the interaction between CDs and Pb2+. Combined with the preceding observation of the morphology, the interaction not only reduces crystal defects but also influences crystallization [26].

We propose a crystal growth mechanism for perovskite films based on the above results. As depicted by the La Mer nucleation theory, the crystallization process of perovskites could be described as nucleation and growth process. When the CDs were mixed in precursor, there are interaction between the CDs and the precursor, which slows down the rate of crystal growth leading to larger grain sizes [12]. Nevertheless, excessive CDs at grain boundaries inhibit grain coalescence, resulting in smaller grains [27,28]. Although the IE also leads to enlarged grain size, the effect mechanism is different from the PE. Crystallization of IE perovskites depends on the nucleation of the underlying substrate for morphology and structure [29]. Several studies have confirmed that the coordination effect between −NH2 groups and perovskite colloidal particles accelerates nucleation on substrates [30,31]. Our results indicate that CDs provide a stronger electrostatic attraction force than SnO2 substrates, which induces preferential colloidal particle sedimentation. The CDs accelerate perovskite nucleation. Meanwhile, the amines on the substrates act as templates to ensure the vertical growth of the crystals, causing greater crystallize orientation and larger grain sizes. Basically, a large number of defects are distributed at grain boundaries, which could be carriers trap and non-radiative recombination center [32,33]. Previous researches indicated that larger grains could reduce trap density and prevent recombination between carriers [34,35]. The dramatic differences between IE and PE devices cannot, however, be entirely explained by morphology and crystallinity. Considering the above-mentioned device performance, we selected a concentration of 0.03 mg/mL for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CDs on perovskite carrier dynamics
ce:section.ce:para:
To further investigate the different impacts of PE and IE, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out. As suggested in Fig. 3e, the IE and PE perovskite films exhibit increased PL peak intensities. Meanwhile, the PL peak of the IE, PE, and control films are located at 763.6, 767.4, and 766.1 nm, respectively. According to the blue-shifted peak position of the IE sample, we deduce that trap states are reduced by the interface engineering. Time-resolved photoluminescence technique (TRPL) was also used to investigate carrier dynamics. TRPL spectra for PE, IE, and control films deposited on ITO substrates are shown in Fig. 3f. The decay curves were fitted by a bi-exponential model, and the corresponding fitting parameters are listed in Table S1. Typically, the faster decay time τ1 is related to non-radiative recombination caused by carrier trap states, whereas the slower decay time τ2 is related to radiation recombination [18,36]. The IE film suggests the largest τ1 value and the smallest fraction of f
1 among all three samples. The average PL lifetimes (τaverage) of IE, PE, and control films are 483.46 ns, 52.52 ns, and 232.24 ns, respectively. The longer average lifetimes indicate less non-radiative recombination. According to the steady-state PL and TRPL results, the CDs at the interface reduced trap-state density and suppressed nonradiative recombination. On the contrary, the PE film exhibits a decreased value of τ1 and an increased fraction of f
1 , leading to a significant decrease in τaverage. It prompted us to conduct further researches.

The energy level diagram indicates that carrier extraction in PE device need overcome extra energy level barriers. Using admittance spectroscopy at 5 MHz and between 0 V and 1.2 V, we further confirmed the energy transfer barriers in the devices. As shown in Fig. S2a in supporting information, PE decreased the built-in potential from 0.90 to 0.88 V. The smaller electrical field (Vbi) indicates a reduced carrier separation in the depletion region of the solar cell [40]. Therefore, although the engineering that CDs dispersed in precursor could enlarge grain sizes and passivate defects, the mismatched energy level causes charge transfer barriers hampering carrier extraction, leading to energy losses. In contrast, the IE device displays a stronger built-in electric field (0.92 V) than the control device (0.90 V). An enhanced electric field and widened depletion region in the IE device are of benefit to promoting carrier extraction, which boosts device performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Device physics of IE-derived solar cells
ce:section.ce:para:
Additional characterizations of control and IE perovskite solar cells were carried out to gain a deeper understanding of how IE contributes to device performance. The steady-state output of the IE and control device was measured by applying a bias voltage at the maximum power output point. As shown in Fig. 5a, the control device provided a steady output power of 16.84 mW/cm2, with bias values of 0.84 V and photocurrents of 20.04 mA/cm2. The IE perovskite device maintained a steady output power of 18.41 mW/cm2, with a bias of 0.86 V and a photocurrent of 21.31 mA/cm2. These results clearly demonstrate the improvement of IE-device performance. Fig. 5b compares the external quantum efficiency (EQE) curves of the IE and control PSCs. The higher EQE in the short wavelength range indicates a more efficient photon-to-electron conversion caused by less nonradiative recombination and better electron extraction. The integrated current densities derived from the EQE spectra for control and IE PSCs are 20.19 mA/cm2 and 21.62 mA/cm2, respectively, which are in good agreement with the J
SC obtained from the J–V curves. Fig. 5c shows that IE and control PSCs have excellent repeatability based on their PCE distributions.

According to the EQE curves, the increased J
SC could be attributed to the more efficient electron extraction at the buried interface. To characterize carrier extraction at buried interface, TRPL measurement was carried out by measuring perovskite deposited on the ETL. Fig. 5d shows the TRPL curves of perovskite films on SnO2 and SnO2/CD substrates. The shorter τ1 and τaverage indicate that IE promotes electron extraction, in agreement with KPFM results at the buried interface [41,42]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted at 10 Hz ∼ 1 MHz bias in the dark. Fig. 5e shows Nyquist plots of the IE and control devices. The curves were fitted through equivalent circuit simulating, and the fitted parameters are listed in Table S3 in Supporting Information. IE device suggests a lower transfer resistance (R
S ) and a higher composite resistance (R
rec ) benefitting to the facilitation of electron extraction and the inhibition of carrier interface recombination [43]. In order to quantitatively evaluate the effect of IE, electron-only devices with a structure of ITO/SnO2/MAPbI3/PCBM/Ag were fabricated. Fig. 5f shows the dark I–V curves of electron-only devices. There is an ohmic response in the linear region of low bias voltage. And it is followed by an intermediate region of current injection caused by trap filling. The trap-fill limit voltage (V
TFL ) can be used to estimate trap state density (n
t ) using the equation below: [44,45].
(1)
V
TFL
=
e
n
t
L
2
/
2 ε
ε
0
where ε is the dielectric constant of MAPbI3, ε
0 is the vacuum permittivity, L is the thickness of MAPbI3, n
t is the trap density, and e represents electronic charge. The trap state density of the IE device and control device were calculated as 2.29 × 1016, and 1.03 × 1016 cm−3, respectively. So, it is proven that IE is capable of passivating defects in devices, which is consistent with the carrier dynamics and morphology mentioned earlier.

The unencapsulated solar cells were fabricated in N2-filled glove box and then placed inside a dry quartz chamber, where they were stored in an ambient environment with constant relative humidity (RH) (10 %). Fig. 6 suggests the shelf stability of the PE, IE and control devices. The PE and IE-device maintained 75 % of its initial efficiency after 22 days, while the control device only provides 60 % of its initial efficiency. Although PE-device exhibited lower PCE than the control device, but the precursor engineering still promoted the device stability. The alleviated decline of PCE could be attributed to the decreased grain boundary and defect passivation.

We also fabricated the PSCs based on Cs0.05(MA0.17FA0.83)0.95Pb (Br0.17I0.83)3 perovskites to verify the universality of CD interface engineering. Fig. S4 in Supporting Information shows the J-V curves of mixed cation perovskite solar cells. A significant improvement in PCE was suggested for mixed-cation PSCs based on IE, increasing from 19.4 % to 21.2 %. The improved performance demonstrates the effectiveness of the CD interface engineering strategy for improving the performance of mixed-cation PSCs.

In this work, amine-terminated carbon dots (CDs) are used in both precursor engineering (PE) and interface engineering (IE) to passivate CH3NH3PbI3 films. And the CDs via PE and IE suggest contrary effects on power conversion efficiency.
i)
According to SEM and XRD results, perovskite films have been enhanced in both grain size and crystallinity. Furthermore, a combination of XPS and FTIR analyses proves that CDs interact with Pb2+ to enlarge grain size and passivate defects. The mechanism for crystallization in PE and IE has been proposed. The presence of CDs slows down the crystal growth rate in PE and induces preferential nucleation of perovskites in IE, thereby increasing grain size in both cases.
ii)
However, the carrier dynamic differs between PE- and IE-derived perovskites. PE leads to PL quenching and faster PL decay. In contrast, IE promotes PL and extends the carrier lifetime. Based on UPS characterization of perovskite, PE leads to an n-type doping effect and exacerbates energy level mismatching, resulting in a narrowed depletion region and undesirable nonradiative recombination. The n-type doping also reduces hole mobility. The IE reduces only the work function of the perovskite at the interface, thus accelerating electron extraction without exacerbating energy mismatches.
iii)
Further electrical tests confirm that IE improves performance. This study contributes to building a comprehensive understanding of defect passivation, morphology, energy level alignment, and mobility of carriers for future development of perovskite and paves the way for optoelectronic devices to benefit from CDs.
Zhao Luo complete data curation and original draft, Xiaoyu Zhang revised and polished the manuscript, and all authors contributed to the analysis of experiment results and revising the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972136 and 52072141).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.172340.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

Supplementary material
.
